summary: I was part of the group that helped start the Washington State Rainbow Coalition in 1984 to support Reverend Jesse Jackson's presidential campaign. The coalition grew out of anti-racist, anti-imperialist, and pro-liberation activism in Seattle and across Washington state. We worked to mobilize a multiracial group of activists and got involved in Democratic Party precinct caucuses and conventions to promote progressive politics. By 1988, the Rainbow Coalition had become a powerful force, securing nearly half of the delegates in Washington supporting Jesse Jackson.

I spent my early years in the suburbs of New York City and attended Columbia University there during 1970-71. While in New York City, I was active in the anti-Vietnam War movement, which was very vibrant at the time. My time in the city helped shape my political awareness before I transferred to Antioch College. New York City was a formative place for me politically and educationally.

I attended Columbia University in New York City for my first year of college in 1970-71. During that time, I was deeply involved in the anti-Vietnam War movement, which was very active on campus and in the city. After that year, I transferred to Antioch College in Ohio to complete my education. Columbia was where I first engaged seriously with protest politics.

I transferred to Antioch College in Yellow Springs, Ohio, after my year at Columbia and spent three years there, graduating with a major in elementary education. Antioch's work-study program allowed me to do an internship in Seattle in 1973, which influenced my decision to move there after graduation. The college was near Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, where I participated in anti-war protests. Antioch was pivotal in shaping both my education and early activism.
cues: anti-Vietnam War protests at Wright-Patterson AFB

summary: I moved to Seattle in 1974 after graduating from Antioch College because I liked the youth movement and progressive activism happening here. In Seattle, I got involved in international solidarity work focused on Central America, anti-racist organizing, and anti-apartheid campaigns. The city was a hub for activism against white supremacist groups like the Ku Klux Klan and for supporting liberation movements in Nicaragua and El Salvador. Seattle shaped much of my political work through the 70s and 80s, including the founding of the Washington State Rainbow Coalition.

I grew up in Nassau County on Long Island, where my parents were active in the Democratic Party. My father was elected as a state representative to the New York State Legislature from Nassau County when I was in elementary school and served for ten years. Growing up there during the Civil Rights and anti-Vietnam War movements influenced my early political awareness. The political environment in Nassau County and my family's involvement gave me insight into electoral politics from a young age.
cues: father elected to New York State Legislature

summary: My father served as a Democratic assemblyman in the New York State Legislature for ten years starting when I was in elementary school. His role gave me a firsthand look at how state government and electoral politics function. This experience informed my understanding of politics even as I engaged more in protest movements during my youth. The legislature was part of my early political environment growing up in New York.

I grew up during the height of the Civil Rights Movement, which deeply influenced the political climate of my youth. Though I was more involved in anti-war protests as a teenager, the Civil Rights Movement's push for racial justice was an important backdrop. Later, my activism would focus heavily on anti-racist organizing, which was rooted in the lessons of the Civil Rights era. The movement shaped the progressive politics I embraced throughout my life.

I was actively involved in protesting the Vietnam War, especially during my high school years and my first year at Columbia University in 1970-71. I participated in protests near Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Ohio while attending Antioch College. Even after moving to Seattle in 1974, I remained engaged as the war was winding down, largely due to the anti-war movement's pressure. The Vietnam War protests were a central part of my early political activism.

While attending Antioch College near Dayton, Ohio, I regularly participated in protests outside the gates of Wright-Patterson Air Force Base. These protests were part of the broader anti-Vietnam War movement. The base was a symbol of US military involvement, and our activism there was an important part of my college years. It was one of the first places where I engaged in organized protest against US foreign policy.

After moving to Seattle, I became involved in international solidarity movements focused on Central America, particularly supporting liberation efforts in Nicaragua and El Salvador. This work connected me to broader anti-imperialist and anti-fascist activism. Through these efforts, I also engaged in anti-racist organizing locally. Central America was a key focus of my activism in the mid to late 1970s and 1980s.

I was involved in solidarity work supporting the liberation movements in Nicaragua while living in Seattle during the 1970s and 80s. This was part of a larger effort to oppose US support for fascist dictatorships in Central America. Our group lobbied elected officials to end US backing of these regimes. Nicaragua was a central focus of our international activism during that period.

Alongside Nicaragua, I worked on solidarity campaigns for liberation movements in El Salvador during the late 1970s and 1980s. We aimed to raise awareness and pressure US politicians to stop supporting the Salvadoran dictatorship. This was part of my broader anti-imperialist and anti-racist activism in Seattle. El Salvador was a key country in the Central American solidarity efforts I participated in.

I was a member of the National Anti-Racist Organizing Committee in Seattle for several years. This group focused on educating the public about the Ku Klux Klan and other white supremacist organizations active in Washington and Idaho. We organized counter-protests and lobbied elected officials to take a stand against racism and white supremacy. The committee also engaged in anti-apartheid activism and international solidarity work during the late 1970s and 1980s.

The Ku Klux Klan was active in Washington state and Idaho during the time I was involved in anti-racist organizing. Through the National Anti-Racist Organizing Committee, we worked to expose their presence and threat through education and counter-protests. We also lobbied elected officials to oppose the Klan and other white supremacist groups. The Klan's activities motivated much of our anti-racist activism in the region.

Idaho was one of the states where white supremacist groups, including the Ku Klux Klan and another right-wing organization, were active during my years of activism. Our anti-racist organizing in Washington also extended to educating people about these threats in Idaho. We worked to raise awareness and lobby officials to confront these groups. Idaho's proximity made it relevant to our regional anti-racist efforts.

Part of our international solidarity work involved opposing US support for fascist dictatorships, including the regime in the Philippines. We lobbied Congress members to end this support as part of our broader anti-imperialist activism. The Philippines was one of several countries where we sought to raise awareness about US foreign policy's negative impacts. This work was connected to our anti-racist and pro-democracy efforts in Seattle.

I was deeply involved in the anti-apartheid movement in Seattle during the late 1970s and 1980s. We formed a Seattle anti-apartheid network that organized protests and divestment campaigns targeting companies doing business in South Africa. We regularly picketed the South African consul's residence in the Madison Park neighborhood, engaging in civil disobedience every Sunday. This was a major focus of the National Anti-Racist Organizing Committee's work during that era.

Madison Park in Seattle was the site of weekly protests and civil disobedience against apartheid during the late 1970s and 1980s. The South African consul operated out of his home there, and our group picketed his residence every Sunday. This location became a focal point for the anti-apartheid movement in Seattle, where I was actively involved. It symbolized our commitment to international justice and local activism.

I was involved in supporting Reverend Jesse Jackson's presidential campaigns in 1984 and 1988 through the Washington State Rainbow Coalition. His progressive, anti-racist, and pro-democracy platform aligned closely with the activism I had been part of for years. I helped organize delegate campaigns within the Democratic Party to elect supporters of Jesse Jackson at every level, from precinct caucuses to the national convention. His candidacy was a major catalyst for multiracial political organizing in Washington state.

I became involved in the Democratic Party in Washington state as part of the strategy to support Jesse Jackson's presidential campaigns. I joined the 37th Legislative District Democratic Party and worked to get delegates elected who supported progressive, anti-racist politics. Our Rainbow Coalition built influence within the party by running candidates for precinct caucuses and leadership positions. This engagement helped increase participation by people of color and progressives in electoral politics.

My activism has always been rooted in the political and social context of the United States, from protesting the Vietnam War to fighting racism and supporting progressive electoral politics. I grew up in New York, moved to Ohio for college, and then settled in Seattle, all within the US. Throughout the 1970s and 80s, I engaged with national issues like civil rights, anti-war protests, and presidential campaigns. The US political system, including its parties and elections, has been the arena for much of my work.

In 1984, I helped form the Washington State Rainbow Coalition to support Jesse Jackson's first presidential campaign. We organized within the Democratic Party to elect delegates committed to Jackson at precinct caucuses and conventions. That year was foundational for learning the political process and building a multiracial coalition in Washington. Our efforts laid the groundwork for increased influence in subsequent elections.

By the 1988 presidential election, the Washington State Rainbow Coalition had grown significantly, and we were able to secure nearly half of the state's delegates supporting Jesse Jackson. We had spent the previous four years building strong grassroots and party organization. The 1988 campaign was a high point for progressive, anti-racist politics in Washington state, and I played a key role in organizing and strategizing within the Democratic Party. It was a major success for the coalition and our movement.

I joined the 37th Legislative District Democratic Party as part of the strategy to support Jesse Jackson's presidential campaigns and to promote progressive politics. Along with others in the Rainbow Coalition, we worked within this district to run candidates for precinct caucuses and to elect delegates committed to Jackson. This district was one of the places where we built political power and increased participation by people of color. It was a key site for our electoral organizing efforts in Seattle.
